summary: The rhythm of cuts is the heartbeat of the ad. Cut length, transition type, and cut-to-beat alignment shape attention density and emotional momentum far more than any single shot.
principles: Cut length equals attention density: shorter cuts create urgency and excitement, longer cuts create absorption and trust.
Match cuts create visual continuity across disparate subjects — the viewer's eye tracks the shared shape, color, or motion across the cut.
L-cuts let audio lead the visual transition so the viewer anticipates the next image; J-cuts let the visual arrive before its audio context for surprise and dynamism.
Hard cuts at peak intensity convey confidence and energy; dissolves and fades at transitions convey passage of time or emotional shift.
The rhythm of cuts should mirror the emotional intensity curve, not fight it — rapid cuts during a calm beat feel anxious, long holds during a peak feel sluggish.
Every cut should have a reason: motivated by motion, dialogue, sound, or emotional shift. Unmotivated cuts feel arbitrary.
The final cut before the CTA should be the cleanest and most decisive in the entire ad — it sets up the conversion moment.
apply_when: The ad has multiple scenes or shots requiring assembly.
The brief specifies pacing energy, mood shifts, or montage treatment.
The emotional curve has distinct intensity levels that editing rhythm should reflect.
avoid_when: The ad is a single continuous shot or animation with no cuts.
Platform constraints (short format) limit the number of viable cuts.
The product requires uninterrupted viewing time to demonstrate its action.
failure_patterns: Cut rhythm at odds with the emotional curve: rapid cuts in a calm section or long holds at the climax.
Every cut is a hard cut with no variation — the ad feels like a slideshow rather than a flowing piece.
Match cuts that are too clever: the viewer notices the technique instead of the product message.
Cutting on the wrong frame: the motion or expression at the cut point is mid-action, creating visual stutter.

cross_domain_notes.note: Cut density should directly track intensity: held shots at low intensity, rapid cuts at peak. They are the visual expression of the same curve.
Camera movement enables or blocks cut types: a tracking shot enables match-cut continuation, a locked shot enables clean hard cuts.
Audio transitions should lead or lag visual cuts by 2-3 frames to create the L-cut/J-cut effect that professional editing relies on.

execution_techniques: Cut on motion when transitioning between beats — the viewer's eye follows the movement and doesn't register the cut.
Use L-cuts to let narration flow across scene boundaries so the voice carries the viewer through the visual change.
Hold the product hero shot 0.5 seconds longer than feels comfortable — it reads as confidence and gives the viewer time to absorb.
Build a cut rhythm pattern in the first 3 seconds (e.g., 1s-1s-1s) then break it at the reveal for maximum impact.
